ashtavakra.profile: Likely identical to the holy sage from the Mahabharata epic, representing the transcendency of the physical body over pure awareness.

janaka.profile: Depicted as a king who attained complete perfection in the Vedas. Identified by some scholars as the father of Sita and direct disciple of sage Yajnavalkya in the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad.

scholarlyDatingDebate.context: Placed immediately after the historical period of the Bhagavad Gita.
Argues it predates Gaudapada because it contains the unformed seed of Ajata Vada (the ultimate theory of non-creation).

scholarlyDatingDebate.designation: Emeritus Professor of Sanskrit, University of Edinburgh
scholarlyDatingDebate.context: Attributes authorship either to a direct follower of Adi Shankara or during the 14th-century Vedantic resurgence.
